## Introduction and approve agenda

Agenda approved. Rodrigo noted that the agenda had been posted the previous week and amended the day before the meeting to correct a mistake flagged by Michael.

Going forward, Rodrigo committed to a new agenda cadence: he will post the TSC meeting agenda (as a PR) four weeks in advance — on the Tuesday before the preceding status meeting — leave it open for comment so any member can propose additions, amend it based on that input, and merge it on the Monday before the TSC meeting.

## Review action items from previous meeting

### CODEOWNERS and GOVERNANCE update ([liboqs PR #2410](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/pull/2410))

Rodrigo reported that five algorithms already present in liboqs still lack an assigned responsible maintainer. He has reached out again to each team and has received responses from three of them (e.g., FrodoKEM). A draft PR incorporating the responses received to date will be prepared. The Falcon team is a special case: their plans depend on the forthcoming NIST IPD, so Rodrigo is in contact with Thomas (their lead point of contact) to understand timing and will share more once known. If no answer arrives from the remaining teams, those algorithms' entries will be updated and outreach will continue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Prepare a draft PR updating CODEOWNERS/GOVERNANCE with the responses received so far, and continue following up with non-responding algorithm teams.

### TSC vice chair role ([issue #295](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/issues/295))

Rodrigo wants to resolve the vice chair question this month so the chair / vice chair structure is in place for the transition to sole-chair mode in September. No one else came forward for the role during the meeting. Douglas reiterated his willingness to serve as vice chair, and also his willingness to step aside if anyone else wanted it. Michael suggested that, since the issue has been open for some time with no other interest, it should simply go to an online vote for Douglas rather than waiting longer. Rodrigo will put it to a vote this week.

> [!IMPORTANT]

## Round 3 on-ramp — algorithm inclusion strategy

Including all remaining round 3 signature on-ramp algorithms in liboqs (and OQS more broadly) is one of the most important objectives for the rest of the year. Of the 9 shortlisted algorithms, liboqs currently has 4; 5 remain. Rodrigo and Douglas outlined a three-phase plan:

**Phase 1 (summer) — preparation, ~100% on the OQS team.** Focus on making inclusion as easy as possible: update documentation, automate what can be automated, and clarify the main blocker points (e.g., the common liboqs API, the DRBG API that submission code must adapt to). Rodrigo cautioned against investing effort in the algorithm implementations themselves right now, since those are expected to change ahead of the round 3 resubmission.

**Phase 2 (September–December) — work with submission teams.** Rodrigo proposed organizing a workshop with all submission teams in early September (after the round 3 submission deadline), modelled on the HQC walkthrough, to explain the inclusion process, point to prior examples (MQOM, CROSS), and highlight likely blockers. He has already contacted all submission teams; three have responded (thanks to Basil for his), with QRUV and FAST keen to collaborate, and two teams yet to respond.

**Dedicated team.** Beyond general participation, Rodrigo, Basil, Bruce, Michael, and the new University of Waterloo hire (starting September) will focus on this task; anyone else is welcome to join.

Discussion points:

- **copy_from_upstream is the recommended path.** Prompted by Michael, the group converged on telling submission teams to use `copy_from_upstream`, with ML-DSA-native (fully upstream-based) and MQOM as worked examples. Bruce confirmed the tool is in a usable state today. Later in the meeting Basil confirmed that updating an upstream integration is simply pointing the algorithm's YAML file at a new branch/commit and re-running the tool, and that API mismatches are handled by glue code in the upstream project's integration subdirectory — reinforcing `copy_from_upstream` as a reusable pattern for the signature teams.
- **Documentation.** Basil pointed to the [`copy_from_upstream` README](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/liboqs/blob/main/scripts/copy_from_upstream/README.md) added alongside the MQOM integration as a starting point; it is probably incomplete and should be revisited/extended. Rodrigo found it a reasonable starting point but felt the biggest gap is documenting the blockers integrators hit (common API, DRBG API). Bruce suggested first documenting a schema for the external `meta.yaml` data sheets and better advertising under-documented features (e.g., placing duplicate files in common folders, which cut the MQOM PR down from ~170,000 files) — an independent improvement that can ship regardless of the larger refactor. Bruce also noted that longer-term (August/September) work is needed to remove legacy assumptions baked in from the PQClean era.
- **Target.** Michael proposed aiming to have a complete-enough documentation set and be ready to run the workshop by the next TSC meeting; documentation need not be perfect since the team will help teams directly.
- **External library dependencies.** Basil flagged that upstream implementations depending on a third-party library cannot be handled automatically by `copy_from_upstream` and add steps (SQIsign plans to remove its dependency for the next round).
- **Mentorships and labels.** Norman offered to join the team and try integrating one of the algorithms himself. On the parallel topic of the Trail of Bits draft PRs (below), the group agreed to tag lower-priority / community-suitable work with the `help wanted` label to signal it is not currently being worked on, and Rodrigo reminded members that the LF mentorship program is another avenue for attracting help.

Douglas thanked Rodrigo for the substantial background email effort with all the submission teams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Document the three-phase inclusion plan (meeting minutes deemed sufficient by Norman) and decide where a fuller writeup should live.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (OQS team, over the summer):** Improve `copy_from_upstream` documentation and tooling — including a documented schema for `meta.yaml` data sheets and better-advertised features — to make the process as easy as possible ahead of the September workshop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Organize an early-September workshop with the submission teams and follow up with the two teams that have not yet responded.

## Security response process

Douglas reported on several related items ([TSC issue #293](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/issues/293)):

**Security email list.** Following discussion in issue #293, Douglas will remove the `security@openquantumsafe.org` email alias from being publicly advertised as an intake path, while keeping the alias itself active (not disabled) as a fallback. Reporters will be directed to the public GitHub security advisory form instead.

**Private security-tracking repository.** Douglas created a private [`security-tracking`](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/security-tracking) repository within the OQS organization, with automation that creates an issue for each security advisory received and project tracking boards. This gives a global view across repositories and a private space for internal discussion (not visible to reporters), tagging, and project management, which GitHub security advisories handle poorly (including different, weaker notifications, as Rodrigo noted). This is a rough first draft and feedback on the organization is welcome. Christian reported a 404 when accessing the repository, so not all TSC members yet have access; Douglas will configure permissions via the TSC config file.

**Classifying reports / intake language.** Michael observed that many recent "security reports" — particularly on oqs-provider — are not genuine security issues but denial-of-service-type findings that would normally be filed as issues or bug reports, often with inflated severity estimates (Basil and Rodrigo agreed; Rodrigo cited a provider report self-rated 9 that he does not consider a security issue). The group agreed to use the upcoming SECURITY.md changes not only to remove the email address but also to clarify *what* should be reported as a security vulnerability versus a normal issue, steering denial-of-service-type findings toward regular issues. Douglas will make these changes for liboqs and Michael for oqs-provider.

**Security process ownership.** Rodrigo raised revisiting how reports are handled — whether to continue the previously discussed rotating "quarterback" model or, now that the tracking repository exists, have each subproject's maintainers take charge of the issues reported against their code. This was flagged for future discussion.

**Trail of Bits engagement.** As part of the OpenAI-sponsored "Patch the Planet" initiative, a Trail of Bits engineer spent a week (roughly two weeks before this meeting) working on OQS; Bruce followed it most closely. Two sets of deliverables resulted:

1. A long report of ~20–30 reported vulnerabilities / hardening items / possible undefined behaviors in liboqs-owned or common code. Rodrigo estimated at least ~50% relate to LMS/XMSS, with some concerning liboqs's own common code (e.g., context strings). Neither Bruce nor Rodrigo has yet vetted the findings for validity or severity. Because the original report link was public, its distribution was held for a couple of days pending this security discussion. The group agreed (Michael's suggestion) to place the report in the new private `security-tracking` repository, then map each item to a tracked issue assigned to whoever owns the relevant code, verify each claim, and decide how to handle it. The goal is to have at least a one-line status on each item by the next TSC meeting.
2. A set of testing/validation infrastructure improvements as four or five liboqs pull requests (fuzzing tests, new mutation testing). Bruce has not yet read into them deeply. The group agreed these are lower priority than the `copy_from_upstream` work, which should be the top priority over the summer; the draft PRs should be tagged `help wanted`.

**OpenAI subscriptions.** A further outcome of the Trail of Bits engagement is the availability of a few sponsored six-month OpenAI Pro subscriptions. Bruce has already taken one up; a few remain. Anyone who would benefit should contact Douglas for the link.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Douglas):** Configure permissions so Christian and all other TSC members have access to the private `security-tracking` repository.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Douglas):** Remove the security email alias from public intake documentation (keeping it active as a fallback) and update intake-path references accordingly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Douglas / Michael):** Update SECURITY.md — Douglas in liboqs, Michael in oqs-provider — to remove the email address and clarify what qualifies as a security report versus a normal issue, steering denial-of-service-type findings toward regular issues.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (all — future discussion):** Decide the ongoing security-handling model (rotating "quarterback" vs. per-subproject maintainer ownership) now that the tracking repository exists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Bruce):** Upload the Trail of Bits report to the private `security-tracking` repository.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Map each Trail of Bits report item to a tracked issue assigned to the relevant code owner, aiming for a one-line status on each by the next TSC meeting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (all):** Anyone wanting a sponsored six-month OpenAI Pro subscription should email Douglas for the link.

## Q2 OQS health report discussion

Rodrigo generated the [Q2 (June 2026) health report](https://github.com/open-quantum-safe/tsc/blob/main/health-reports/june-2026/report.pdf). Highlights: a notable improvement in oqs-provider (from a low base) and a minor (decimal-point) downgrade for liboqs, within expected activity variation. Rodrigo stressed that the most important part is the recommended actions, grouped into three tiers — critical (address immediately), high priority (within 3 months), and medium term (within 6 months) — and asked members to review them and indicate agreement or disagreement.

When Michael asked who had read the report, only Michael and Rodrigo had. Rodrigo therefore deferred substantive discussion: he will open an issue to track it and the TSC will debate it at the next meeting once all members have had a chance to read it.

Michael, who had read it, endorsed one recommendation: the report flags a single-point-of-failure bus factor (himself) and recommends writing up Architectural Decision Records (ADRs) — capturing the reasoning behind design decisions so people (or AI tools) can understand the project and evaluate/reject contributions accordingly (e.g., an ADR clarifying that OQS is about post-quantum, not new hash algorithms). This aligns with the motivation for his OpenSSL Conference presentation last year. Michael will work on writing up such ADRs and recommended the whole team consider doing the same across the project throughout the year; he specifically suggested Douglas read the report with an eye to what else could be documented this way.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Rodrigo):** Open an issue to track the Q2 health report for discussion at the next TSC meeting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (all members):** Read the Q2 health report — especially the recommended actions — before the next TSC meeting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Action (Michael):** Begin writing up Architectural Decision Records (ADRs) to reduce the bus factor and document design rationale.

### PQ Code Package (Basil)

- **mlkem-native:** A new release landed two weeks ago; most is already merged into liboqs (verification enhancements under the hood). A new PowerPC 64 assembly backend is not yet in liboqs — Basil is working on adding support, including CI.
- **mldsa-native:** All releases so far have been beta; the goal is the first major release (v1.0) this month (July), whose main milestone is being fully formally verified. For liboqs this should just be a pull update with no compatibility change. One discussion of interest to liboqs: a proposal in mldsa-native to slightly change the signature-generation API by removing the redundant/error-prone signature-length pointer (ML-DSA has a fixed signature length). liboqs would keep a compatibility layer since it supports multiple signature schemes, some historically with variable-length signatures — and this serves as a good example of adding integration/glue code to map an upstream API to liboqs. Both mlkem-native and mldsa-native continuously test the liboqs integration in their own CI, which simplifies the process.
- **New project proposal:** A proposal to onboard a tooling project (not a crypto implementation) that automatically applies further architecture-specific optimizations to assembly code — not directly of interest to liboqs, but potentially usable to further optimize the implementations OQS pulls.

### PQCA general / Outreach and Education (Hart)

Not covered — Hart was not present.
